Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Gottlob Frege

1848 – 1925

Thoughts are not ideas: the anti-psychologist who redrew the boundary between mind and logic.

Frege did not write a systematic philosophy of mind in the way Descartes, Brentano, Husserl, Ryle, or contemporary philosophers of consciousness did. His central projects were logic, the foundations of arithmetic, semantics, and the objectivity of thought. Still, a robust philosophy of mind can be extracted from his work. It is not primarily a theory of consciousness as such, nor a doctrine of the soul. It is a theory of the relation between private mental life, objective thought-content, language, truth, and rational normativity.

The core Fregean thesis is this: thinking is a psychological act, but what is thought is not psychological. The mind performs acts: sensing, imagining, associating, grasping, judging, asserting, doubting, believing, hoping, fearing. But the contents that make genuine thinking answerable to truth—Frege’s “thoughts” or Gedanken—are not inner mental images, not subjective experiences, and not products of an individual psyche. They are objective, shareable, non-sensible truth-bearers. Frege’s philosophy of mind is therefore a form of anti-psychologistic semantic realism: minds are needed for thinking, but truth and logical content are not constituted by minds.

Frege’s position is historically pivotal because it redirects part of the philosophy of mind away from the older problem of the soul and toward problems of content, intentionality, belief, cognitive significance, objectivity, and normativity. Frege belongs to the period in which psychology was becoming an independent science, while logic and philosophy were struggling over whether the laws of logic were laws of mental processes. Brentano, for example, defined psychology as the science of mental phenomena and made intentional directedness central to mind; he also treated logic as dependent in important respects on psychology. Frege’s decisive move was the opposite: psychological processes explain how someone comes to take something as true, but logic concerns truth itself, not the mental genesis of acceptance.

1. Historical setting: psychologism and the new science of mind

Frege’s thought develops against the background of nineteenth-century psychologism. Psychologism, in the relevant sense, treats logic, mathematics, meaning, or validity as grounded in facts about mental processes, laws of association, habits of judgment, or the constitution of human cognition. Frege’s work is one of the decisive anti-psychologistic interventions in modern philosophy. He was a German mathematician, logician, and philosopher at Jena, and his formal system helped inaugurate modern predicate logic; but his logical revolution is inseparable from his insistence that logic is not empirical psychology.

The controversy matters for philosophy of mind because it forced a distinction between acts of thinking and contents of thinking. A psychologist may explain why a subject believes a proposition, how an image is associated with a word, how attention works, or how inference actually occurs in time. But such explanations do not determine whether the proposition is true, whether the inference is valid, or whether two speakers grasp the same thought. Frege’s polemic is thus not anti-psychological in the sense of denying mental life; it is anti-psychologistic in the sense of denying that psychology is the foundation of logic, mathematics, or meaning.

Frege’s own texts are more nuanced than the slogan “logic is not psychology” might suggest. In natural thinking, the logical and the psychological are intertwined; the task of logic is to isolate what is logical from the psychological mixture. A recent historical study emphasizes this dual aspect: Frege recognizes that ordinary thinking occurs psychologically, but his philosophical aim is to separate logical justification from psychological occurrence.

That distinction anticipates a major modern division: cognitive science may study the causal mechanisms of reasoning, while logic and semantics study validity, truth, reference, and content. Frege is one of the sources of that division.

2. Frege’s anti-psychologism: truth is not taking-to-be-true

Frege’s most important anti-psychologistic distinction is between truth and being taken to be true. Psychological laws may describe the circumstances under which human beings accept judgments. They may explain why one person reasons correctly, why another falls into error, why a belief is compelling, or why a superstition persists. But such laws cannot replace a proof. They describe mental processes; they do not determine truth or validity. In “The Thought,” Frege explicitly assigns logic the task of discovering the laws of truth, not the laws of thinking as a mental process.

This is the central point from which his philosophy of mind follows. The mind is fallible because psychological causation can produce both truth and error. A belief may arise from perception, memory, training, wishful thinking, bad association, or valid inference. The genesis of belief is one thing; the justification of belief is another. In modern terminology, Frege sharply separates causal explanation from epistemic warrant.

Frege’s criticisms of mathematical psychologism are especially revealing. He rejects the view that mathematics is grounded in subjective ideas, mental pictures, or empirical generalizations. Numbers are not mental images; mathematical truths are not psychological truths; and even if empirical experience helps us learn arithmetic, it does not justify arithmetic. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Frege’s position this way: mathematics and logic are not parts of psychology, and psychological observations cannot define, explain, or prove mathematical truths.

Frege’s objection to “laws of thought” language is subtle. Logical laws may guide how we ought to think, but they are not primarily empirical laws describing how people do think. A physical law can be turned into a prescription—one ought to believe it if it is true—but that does not make physics psychology. Similarly, a logical law can have normative force for thought, but its authority comes from truth, not from facts about human mental habits.

The resulting view is strongly anti-relativist. If truth were identical with what humans generally accept, or with what our species is psychologically compelled to accept, then truth would be hostage to human constitution. Frege rejects that. Logical laws are not validated by consensus, psychology, biology, or anthropology. They are standards by which our thinking is judged.

3. The Fregean map of mind: ideas, thoughts, things

Frege’s mature philosophy works with a threefold distinction:

First, there are things in the external world: trees, planets, bodies, physical objects, public entities perceptible by the senses.

Second, there are ideas (Vorstellungen) in the subjective sense: sense impressions, mental images, sensations, feelings, moods, inclinations, wishes, imaginative contents, and other contents of individual consciousness. These are private, owner-dependent, and psychologically conditioned.

Third, there are thoughts (Gedanken) and other objective, non-sensible contents: the senses of truth-evaluable sentences, the contents that can be true or false, the objects of judgment, proof, and scientific communication.

This tripartite structure is the backbone of Frege’s philosophy of mind. A person sees a thing, has an idea, and grasps a thought. Those are different relations. Seeing is directed toward something in the external world. Having is the ownership of a subjective mental occurrence. Grasping is the cognitive relation to an objective thought. Frege explicitly says that when one grasps a thought one does not create it, but comes into relation with something already there.

This is why Frege’s view is not Cartesian dualism in the ordinary sense. He does not merely divide reality into mind and body. He introduces a third category: objective, non-mental, non-sensible content. In “The Thought,” he says that thoughts are neither external sensible things nor inner ideas, and therefore a “third realm” must be recognized. Thoughts resemble ideas in not being sense-perceptible, but they resemble things in not needing an owner. The thought expressed by the Pythagorean theorem is true independently of whether anyone takes it to be true.

The result is a kind of logical or semantic Platonism, though that label must be used carefully. Frege is realist about objective thoughts, senses, numbers, truth-values, and logical entities, but he is not saying that thoughts are ghostly mental events. They are not in anyone’s head. They are also not physical objects. They are objective contents available to be grasped by minds.

4. Ideas: Frege’s account of private mental life

Frege’s “ideas” are the closest thing in his system to what contemporary philosophers might call subjective experiences, mental images, or phenomenological contents. In “On Sense and Reference,” he describes an idea associated with a sign as an internal image arising from memories of sense impressions and from acts one has performed. Such an idea may be saturated with feeling, may vary in clarity, and may change even within the same person. Most importantly, an idea is subjective: one person’s idea is not another’s.

This gives Frege a clear theory of mental privacy. Two people may associate different images with the same word. A painter, a horseman, and a zoologist may have different associated ideas when hearing the same name. Those private ideas are not the public meaning of the name. The same sense can be grasped by many people, but the same idea cannot be literally had by many people.

Frege’s point is not merely semantic. It is also a theory of subjectivity. Ideas are owned. A pain needs someone who has it. A mood, wish, visual impression, or feeling cannot float free of a subject. The inner world presupposes an owner whose inner world it is. Frege uses pain as a paradigmatic example: the patient owns the pain; the doctor may have an idea of the pain and may think about it, but the doctor does not have the patient’s pain. Two doctors may think about the same patient’s pain, but neither thereby owns that pain.

This is a surprisingly strong anticipation of later discussions of privacy, first-person authority, and phenomenal ownership. Frege is not primarily interested in qualia, but he clearly holds that subjective episodes cannot be literally shared. There may be common objects of thought, but not common private experiences.

5. The self: the “I” is not an idea

Frege rejects a reduction of the self to a bundle of ideas. He asks whether he himself might be merely a complex of visual impressions, bodily sensations, and images. His answer is no. If everything were an idea, there would be no owner of ideas; but ideas require an owner. The “I” is not identical with any idea of myself. When I judge that I am not in pain, the judgment concerns myself, not merely an inner image associated with the word “I.”

This gives Frege a minimal but important theory of the subject. The subject is the bearer or owner of ideas and the performer of acts of thinking, but not itself just another idea among ideas. Frege therefore rejects a Humean or phenomenalist reduction of the self to a stream of impressions. At the same time, he does not develop a full metaphysics of personal substance. He does not argue, in these central texts, for an immortal soul, a Cartesian res cogitans, or a theological doctrine of mind. His point is more constrained: the structure of experience requires an owner, and the owner cannot be reduced to the owned contents.

Frege’s treatment of first-person thought also shows how far his philosophy of mind reaches. He notes that each person is presented to himself in a special primitive way in which he is presented to no one else. This creates a difficulty for communication: if a speaker’s first-person thought were graspable only by that speaker, it could not be communicated. Therefore, ordinary uses of “I” in communication must involve publicly graspable conditions, such as “the person speaking now,” even though self-acquaintance itself has a special first-person character.

This is one of the deepest Fregean points for later philosophy of mind: first-person thought has a distinctive mode of presentation, but communicable language must transform or coordinate that mode with public sense. Frege anticipates later problems about indexicals, self-knowledge, and the irreducibility of “I”-thoughts.

6. Thoughts: objective contents, not mental episodes

Frege’s word Gedanke is easily misleading in English because “thought” often means a mental event. Frege’s “thought” is not the act of thinking. It is the objective content that can be true or false. In “The Thought,” he says that a thought is the kind of thing for which the question of truth can arise; false thoughts count as thoughts just as true ones do. A sentence expresses a thought, and a thought is made accessible through the perceptible sentence that expresses it.

This distinction yields three different levels:

A thought is the objective truth-evaluable content.

Thinking is the act of grasping a thought.

Judgment is the acknowledgment of a thought as true.

Assertion is the outward manifestation of judgment.

Frege explicitly distinguishes these acts: grasping a thought, acknowledging its truth, and manifesting that acknowledgment in assertion. This is a compact but powerful philosophy of cognition. It separates entertaining a content from accepting it, and accepting it from publicly asserting it.

The distinction remains central in contemporary philosophy of mind. One can entertain the proposition that it will rain without believing it. One can believe it without saying it. One can say it insincerely without judging it true. Frege’s account makes these distinctions possible because he refuses to identify content with any particular psychological act.

7. Sense, reference, and cognitive significance

Frege’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his philosophy of language. His famous distinction between sense (Sinn) and reference (Bedeutung) is also a theory of cognitive content.

A name or expression has a reference: the object, function, or truth-value it stands for. But it also has a sense: the way in which the reference is given or presented. “Morning star” and “evening star” have the same reference, Venus, but different senses. That difference explains why identity statements can be cognitively informative. “Venus is Venus” and “the morning star is the evening star” may have the same truth-value, but they express different thoughts.

The mental importance of this is immense. Frege gives an account of how two beliefs can concern the same object while differing in cognitive significance. A subject can believe something under one mode of presentation while failing to believe it under another. This is one of Frege’s foundational contributions to the philosophy of intentionality and belief.

Sense is not an idea. Ideas are private psychological accompaniments. Sense is objective and can be grasped by many. Frege explicitly places sense “in between” reference and subjective idea: it is not the object itself, but it is not a private mental image either.

Thus, a Fregean philosophy of mind distinguishes:

Phenomenal accompaniment: the private image, feeling, or association a subject has.

Mode of presentation: the objective sense under which an object is thought.

Reference: the object, function, or truth-value referred to.

This gives Frege a sophisticated alternative to both crude mentalism and crude referentialism. Meaning is not an image in the head. But neither is cognitive content exhausted by external reference.

8. Belief, propositional attitudes, and intentionality

Frege’s account of belief reports is one of the most important bridges from philosophy of language to philosophy of mind. In ordinary extensional contexts, a sentence refers to a truth-value. But in indirect speech and propositional attitude contexts—such as “John believes that…,” “Mary hopes that…,” “Wellington was glad that…”—the embedded sentence does not function in the ordinary way. Its reference becomes the thought it ordinarily expresses.

This explains why substitution of co-referential expressions can fail in belief contexts. Someone may believe that Mark Twain wrote a novel without believing that Samuel Clemens did, even if Mark Twain is Samuel Clemens. The two names have the same reference but different senses; the believer may grasp one thought and not the other. The Stanford Encyclopedia presents this as Frege’s solution to the breakdown of simple substitution in propositional attitude reports.

For philosophy of mind, this is crucial. Frege gives a theory of intentional mental states as relations to thoughts under senses. Belief is not just a relation to an object; it is a relation to an object as presented in a particular way. The object of belief is not merely the truth-value, and not merely the external thing, but a structured thought.

Frege’s account also touches emotion. In “On Sense and Reference,” he says that hope, fear, regret, approval, and similar attitudes involve convictions; for example, Wellington’s gladness that the Prussians were coming depends on a conviction, and if he had been deceived his gladness would still be explained by that believed thought.

This does not amount to a full cognitive theory of emotion, but it shows that Frege treats at least many emotions as content-involving. The intentional content of such an emotion is not a private image; it is a thought grasped or taken as true by the subject.

9. Perception: sense impressions are necessary but not sufficient

Frege’s remarks on perception are scattered but philosophically rich. He acknowledges the physiological story: light, retinas, nerve stimulation, and sense impressions. But he argues that sense impressions alone do not reveal the external world. To have visual impressions is not yet to see a thing. Two people may have different retinal images and different sense impressions, yet both see the same tree in the same external world.

This is a strong anti-sense-datum point. The object of seeing is not a private visual impression. The visual impression is part of the inner world; the perceived tree is not. If we treated the impression as the only object of awareness, we would collapse into idealism or solipsism: we would no longer have access to external objects or other persons.

Frege therefore holds that perception involves more than private sensory material. He says that something non-sensible must be added if the external world is to be opened to us. This “non-sensible something” is one of the most enigmatic points in Frege’s philosophy. It does not mean that perception is mystical. It means that object-directed awareness cannot be reduced to sensory impressions considered as private mental contents.

The point aligns Frege with a non-reductive view of perceptual intentionality. Seeing is not merely having an image; it is being related to an object. Similarly, thinking is not merely having an image; it is grasping a thought. Both perception and thought require the mind to reach beyond what it privately owns.

10. Language as the public vehicle of thought

Language is essential because thoughts are not sense-perceptible. A thought must be expressed in a perceptible form—a sentence, inscription, sound, gesture, or sign—so that it can be grasped and communicated. This does not make thought linguistic in the crude sense that thoughts are marks or noises. Rather, language is the public vehicle through which non-sensible content becomes accessible to finite thinkers.

Frege also distinguishes thought from the psychological and aesthetic accompaniments of language. Tone, rhythm, mood, poetic atmosphere, emotional coloring, and associative imagery may affect a hearer, but they do not belong to the thought in the strict logical sense. Natural language contains more than truth-evaluable content; it also carries feelings, images, hints, moods, and pragmatic suggestions. The logician must isolate what is relevant to truth and inference.

This is why Frege valued formal notation. His concept-script was meant to remove ambiguity and psychological distraction from the expression of logical relations. But he did not think ordinary language should be replaced everywhere. Ordinary language is flexible and connected with mental life; formal notation is a specialized instrument for exact scientific and logical purposes.

So Frege’s philosophy of mind includes a theory of communication: people influence one another by producing changes in the external world—sounds, marks, gestures—that allow others to grasp thoughts. Thoughts do not pass like physical objects from one mind to another; they remain what they are, while different thinkers come to grasp them.

11. The third realm and mental causation

The third realm creates an apparent problem: if thoughts are timeless, non-sensible, and not physical, how can they matter to human life?

Frege’s answer is that thoughts act only through being grasped and taken as true. Once a thinker grasps a thought and judges it true, that inner act can affect the will and eventually outward action. A geometrical theorem may influence engineering; a political thought may influence history; a communicated thought may move another person to act. Thoughts do not push bodies like hammers do. Their efficacy is mediated by acts of thinking, judging, willing, and communicating.

This yields a distinctive view of mental causation. The thought itself is not a physical cause in the ordinary sense, but it is not inert either. Its worldly efficacy depends on its being grasped by a thinker. Frege therefore avoids treating thoughts as mental episodes while still allowing them to matter in human action.

This is one of the places where Frege’s view remains underdeveloped. He tells us that thinkers can grasp thoughts, and that thoughts can indirectly influence action through judgment and will. But he gives no naturalistic explanation of how a temporal psychological act can relate to a timeless objective content. That “grasping” relation is indispensable to his view and also its deepest metaphysical pressure point.

12. The soul, incorporeal minds, and divine consciousness

Frege’s central writings do not give us a developed doctrine of the soul. His philosophy is not a theory of immortality, not a theology of mind, and not a Cartesian proof of an immaterial thinking substance. Still, several nearby commitments matter.

First, Frege’s “I” is not reducible to an idea. The self is the owner of ideas and the subject of thinking. This blocks any view that reduces a person to a collection of mental images or sensations. But Frege does not clearly identify the owner with a soul-substance. The metaphysical status of the owner remains spare and functional: it is that which has ideas, thinks, judges, and can take objects as objects of thought.

Second, Frege’s thoughts are incorporeal in a broad sense: they are not physical, not perceptible by the senses, not spatial objects, and not mental episodes. But they are not minds. They are not souls, spirits, or consciousnesses. The “incorporeal” element Frege clearly posits is the objective realm of thought-content, not an incorporeal personal mind.

Third, Frege briefly considers a possibility resembling divine consciousness. In discussing whether two people could literally have the same idea, he asks whether one’s ideas might belong to a more embracing, perhaps divine, consciousness. His response is not to build a doctrine on it but to set it aside as beyond human understanding. This is important: Frege acknowledges the conceptual possibility, but it is not part of his philosophical machinery.

Fourth, Frege does posit a “special mental capacity,” the power of thinking, by which we grasp thoughts. That capacity is not reducible, in his account, to sensory impressions or psychological association. It is the capacity by which finite subjects relate to objective, non-sensible content.

Therefore, the safest conclusion is this: Frege is not a philosopher of the soul, but his philosophy is anti-reductionist about mind. He does not reduce the subject to sensations, does not reduce thought-content to mental imagery, and does not reduce logic to psychological laws. But he also does not give a positive metaphysics of the soul comparable to Plato, Augustine, Aquinas, Descartes, or Leibniz.

13. Extracting Frege’s philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A comprehensive Fregean philosophy of mind can be reconstructed in the following theses.

1. The mind has private inner contents.
Sensations, images, moods, feelings, wishes, pains, and associations belong to individual consciousness. They are owner-dependent and not literally shareable.

2. The self is not one of its ideas.
A subject owns ideas but is not identical with any particular idea, nor with a mere collection of ideas. Experience requires an owner.

3. Thinking is not the production of thoughts.
To think is to grasp an objective thought. The thinker owns the act of thinking, not the thought thought.

4. Judgment is distinct from mere entertaining.
A subject can grasp a thought without judging it true. Judgment is the acknowledgment of truth; assertion is the public manifestation of that judgment.

5. Meaning is not mental imagery.
The sense of an expression is not the private idea associated with it. Sense is objective and shareable; ideas are subjective and variable.

6. Intentionality is mediated by sense.
A mind can think about the same object under different modes of presentation. This explains cognitive difference, informative identity, and opacity in belief reports.

7. Truth is independent of psychology.
Psychology explains taking-to-be-true; logic concerns being true. No fact about human acceptance, consensus, or mental constitution makes a logical law valid.

8. Perception is not reducible to sense impressions.
Visual impressions are inner and private, but seeing is object-directed. The mind can be related to things it does not own.

9. Communication is possible because thoughts are public.
If thoughts were private ideas, shared science, disagreement, proof, and communication would collapse. Public language enables different thinkers to grasp the same thought.

10. The mental is norm-governed because it is truth-directed.
Belief and judgment are answerable to truth. Logic is normative for thought not because it describes psychological regularities, but because thought aims at truth.

11. The ontology is neither simple dualism nor materialism.
Frege’s mature picture is tripartite: external things, inner ideas, and objective thoughts. This is not body-soul dualism; it is a three-level account of world, consciousness, and logical content.

12. The soul is not central.
Frege’s system requires subjects capable of owning ideas and grasping thoughts, but it does not require a developed doctrine of incorporeal souls. The nearest analogue to an incorporeal realm is the third realm of objective thoughts, not a realm of minds.

14. Frege’s historical position in philosophy of mind

Frege’s place in the history of philosophy of mind is indirect but profound.

Against the older “idea” tradition, he denies that meaning and thought-content are private mental images. This marks a decisive break from forms of empiricism and psychologism that treat ideas as the basic units of cognition. Against idealism and solipsism, he argues that objectivity and shared science require common thoughts and common objects, not merely private contents of consciousness.

Against Brentano-style psychologism, Frege relocates intentionality. Brentano made intentional directedness the mark of mental phenomena; Frege instead gives a semantic account of how thought reaches objects through sense and reference. Contemporary philosophy still treats intentionality as a central interface between philosophy of mind and philosophy of language, and Frege is one of the main sources of the language-centered approach to that interface.

Against nineteenth-century psychological logic, he makes logic autonomous. This shapes both analytic philosophy and phenomenology. Frege criticized Husserl’s early philosophy of arithmetic as psychologistic because it treated meanings, concepts, and objects as ideas and gave psychological-genetic accounts of abstract concepts; Husserl’s later anti-psychologism became central to phenomenology, though scholars still debate the exact causal influence of Frege’s criticism on Husserl’s turn.

Within analytic philosophy, Frege’s influence is enormous. His logic and philosophy of language shaped Russell, early Wittgenstein, and later logical positivism; his distinctions between sense and reference, thought and idea, judgment and assertion, and truth and taking-to-be-true became foundational for twentieth-century debates about language, belief, and mind.

For contemporary philosophy of mind, Frege’s legacy appears especially in four areas: the distinction between mental vehicle and intentional content; the analysis of propositional attitudes; the opacity of belief contexts; and the normativity of belief and reasoning. His view also anticipates the distinction between phenomenal privacy and public conceptual content: my sensations and images are private, but the thought I grasp may be the same thought another person grasps.

15. Main tensions and unresolved problems

Frege’s philosophy of mind is powerful, but it leaves several hard problems.

The first is the grasping problem. How can a temporal, psychologically embodied subject grasp an objective, non-sensible, timeless thought? Frege insists that this happens, and his entire account of logic and communication depends on it, but he does not give a satisfying mechanism. Contemporary scholars continue to debate whether Frege’s “third realm” should be read as robust metaphysical Platonism or as a more deflationary way of marking the objectivity of assertion and judgment.

The second is the naturalization problem. Frege’s anti-psychologism protects logic from empirical reduction, but it also distances his theory from cognitive science. Modern philosophers may agree that validity is not a psychological regularity while still wanting an account of how brains or embodied agents grasp abstract contents. Frege gives the normative structure, not the cognitive mechanism.

The third is the first-person problem. Frege recognizes primitive self-presentation and private first-person access, but he also needs thoughts to be communicable. His treatment of “I” shows the tension: some self-presenting modes may be graspable only by the subject, but communication requires public senses.

The fourth is the phenomenal-content problem. Frege sharply separates subjective ideas from objective thoughts. That is useful for logic, but it risks under-theorizing the cognitive role of imagery, affect, embodiment, and perspective. In ordinary mental life, affective and imagistic elements often shape what a thought means to a person, even if they do not belong to the strict logical content.

The fifth is the causal efficacy problem. Frege says thoughts affect the world by being grasped and taken as true. That is plausible at the level of action explanation, but metaphysically delicate: if thoughts are timeless and non-actual in the ordinary causal sense, their relation to temporal mental acts needs clarification.

Conclusion

Frege’s philosophy of mind, reconstructed from his logic and semantics, can be summarized in one sentence:

A mind is a subject with private experiences and psychological acts, capable of grasping objective, shareable thoughts whose truth and logical relations are independent of any mind’s taking them to be true.

This is not a doctrine of the soul. It is not a psychology of consciousness. It is a theory of the objectivity of thought, the privacy of ideas, the normativity of judgment, and the semantic structure of intentionality. Its historical importance lies in shifting philosophy of mind toward questions that still dominate the field: What is mental content? How can different subjects share a thought? How can belief be about an object under one mode of presentation but not another? What distinguishes causal explanation of belief from rational justification? How can private consciousness participate in public truth?

Frege’s answer is austere but deep: psychology studies the inner occurrence of thinking; logic studies the laws of truth; semantics explains the objective contents that make thought communicable; and the human mind stands at the intersection of all three.
